section.title: A Lament for Egypt
section.sectionRationale: This chapter widens the scope of Egypt's judgment to include her allies and pictures a day of the LORD falling on Egypt and everyone who depends on her, naming city after city that will burn or fall by the sword, before closing with a second oracle in which Pharaoh's arm, once raised in strength, is shown already broken and about to be broken further, while Babylon's arm is strengthened in its place.
section.thesis: The day of the LORD comes not only for Israel but for every nation and every ally bound up with a proud power like Egypt, whose broken arm no human effort can heal, showing that trust placed in any strength but God's own is trust placed in something already failing.
section.frameNote: Two oracles: a wide ranging lament naming Egypt's allies and cities in turn as the day of the LORD falls upon them all; and a second, later oracle picturing Pharaoh's arm already broken, soon to be shattered entirely while the LORD strengthens the arm of Babylon's king in its place.

units.label: The day of the LORD is near
units.note: for Egypt and all her helpers

verses.text: Again the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, prophesy and declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: Wail, 'Alas for that day!'
For the day is near, the {{yom-yhwh|Day of the LORD}} is near. It will be a day of clouds, a time of doom for the nations.
A sword will come against Egypt, and there will be anguish in Cush when the slain fall in Egypt, its wealth is taken away, and its foundations are torn down.
Cush, Put, and Lud, and all the various peoples, as well as Libya and the men of the covenant land, will fall with Egypt by the sword.
For this is what the LORD says: The allies of Egypt will fall, and her proud strength will collapse. From Migdol to Syene they will fall by the sword within her, declares the Lord GOD.
They will be desolate among desolate lands, and their cities will lie among ruined cities.
Then they will know that I am the LORD when I set fire to Egypt and all her helpers are shattered.
On that day messengers will go out from Me in ships to frighten Cush out of complacency. Anguish will come upon them on the day of Egypt's doom. For it is indeed coming.
This is what the Lord GOD says: I will put an end to the hordes of Egypt by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon.
He and his people with him, the most ruthless of the nations, will be brought in to destroy the land. They will draw their swords against Egypt and fill the land with the slain.
I will make the streams dry up and sell the land to the wicked. By the hands of foreigners I will bring desolation upon the land and everything in it. I, the LORD, have spoken.

units.label: City after city named
units.note: Memphis, Zoan, Thebes, and the rest

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: I will destroy the idols and put an end to the images in Memphis. There will no longer be a prince in Egypt, and I will instill fear in that land.
I will lay waste Pathros, set fire to Zoan, and execute judgment on Thebes.
I will pour out My wrath on Pelusium, the stronghold of Egypt, and cut off the crowds of Thebes.
I will set fire to Egypt, Pelusium will writhe in anguish, Thebes will be split open, and Memphis will face daily distress.
The young men of On and Pi-beseth will fall by the sword, and those cities will go into captivity.
The day will be darkened in Tahpanhes when I break the yoke of Egypt and her proud strength comes to an end. A cloud will cover her, and her daughters will go into captivity.
So I will execute judgment on Egypt, and they will know that I am the LORD.”
In the eleventh year, on the seventh day of the first month, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, I have broken the {{zroa|arm}} of Pharaoh king of Egypt. See, it has not been bound up for healing, or splinted for strength to hold the sword.

Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: Behold, I am against Pharaoh king of Egypt. I will break his arms, both the strong one and the one already broken, and will make the sword fall from his hand.
I will disperse the Egyptians among the nations and scatter them throughout the lands.
I will strengthen the arms of Babylon's king and place My sword in his hand, but I will break the arms of Pharaoh, who will groan before him like a mortally wounded man.

I will strengthen the arms of Babylon's king, but Pharaoh's arms will fall limp. Then they will know that I am the LORD, when I place My sword in the hand of Babylon's king, and he wields it against the land of Egypt.
I will disperse the Egyptians among the nations and scatter them throughout the lands. Then they will know that I am the LORD.”

yom-yhwh.gloss: The Day of the LORD, a recurring prophetic phrase for a decisive moment when God intervenes in judgment or salvation, most often associated with Israel's own accountability but here, strikingly, applied to Egypt and the nations, showing that this day belongs to every people, not Israel alone.

zroa.gloss: The arm, a common biblical picture of strength, especially military power, as in 'the arm of the LORD' or 'the arm of flesh.' Pharaoh's arm, once a symbol of Egypt's might, is declared already broken and beyond human healing, while the LORD strengthens Babylon's arm in its place.

basic.context: This chapter widens the scope of Egypt's judgment from the previous chapter's focus on Pharaoh himself to include Egypt's regional allies, Cush, Put, Lud, Libya, and names city after city across Egypt, Memphis, Zoan, Thebes, Pelusium, and more, that will fall in the coming devastation, before a second, later oracle pictures Pharaoh's arm as already broken beyond human healing while the LORD deliberately strengthens the arm of Babylon's king against him.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real, historically anchored judgment on Egypt and her actual regional allies and cities, most of which are identifiable ancient Egyptian centers, fulfilled substantially through Babylonian incursions into Egypt in the following decades. The 'Day of the LORD' language, elsewhere often tied to Israel's own accountability, here describes God's real, historical intervention against Egypt specifically, while also carrying the wider prophetic resonance this phrase always holds of God's ultimate reckoning with all nations.
basic.originalLanguages: This is called the yom YHWH, the Day of the LORD, language usually reserved for Israel's own accountability but here turned outward to Egypt, showing that this decisive day belongs to every nation. And Pharaoh's zeroa, his arm, the biblical symbol of military strength, is declared already broken beyond human splinting, while God deliberately strengthens Babylon's arm to wield the sword against him.
basic.typology: Pharaoh's broken arm, beyond any human splint or bandage to heal, anticipates the wider biblical truth that human strength, 'the arm of flesh,' always fails, while only 'the arm of the LORD' truly saves, a phrase Isaiah later applies directly to the coming Messiah, Isaiah 53:1.
basic.christ: Where Pharaoh's arm is broken and cannot be healed by any human effort, Isaiah later asks 'to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed', Isaiah 53:1, answered in Christ, the true and only arm of salvation, whose strength, unlike Pharaoh's, cannot be broken and needs no human splint to hold it.
basic.god: God is Lord of history, deliberately breaking one nation's power while deliberately strengthening another's, even a pagan empire like Babylon, entirely for His own purposes; His sovereignty is not passive but active, personally breaking Pharaoh's arms and personally placing His own sword in Nebuchadnezzar's hand.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 19:1-25 and Jeremiah 46:1-26 give companion oracles against Egypt from the same general period. Isaiah 53:1 asks 'to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed', a phrase this chapter's broken 'arm of Pharaoh' sets in sharp relief. Joel 2:1-2 and Zephaniah 1:14-15 use similar 'day of clouds' and 'Day of the LORD' language for coming judgment on nations generally.
basic.oneStory: Egypt's proud strength and all her allies fall on the day the LORD Himself declares, her king's arm broken beyond human healing while Babylon's arm is strengthened for God's own purposes. This anticipates the whole biblical truth that every human arm of strength eventually fails, while the true arm of the LORD, revealed fully in Christ, alone accomplishes a salvation that cannot be broken.

context.literary: The second of four chapters devoted to Egypt, 29 to 32, combining two separately dated oracles, a wide ranging lament naming Egypt's allies and cities, verses 1 to 19, and a later oracle picturing Pharaoh's broken arm, verses 20 to 26, both developing the previous chapter's themes of Egypt's pride and coming humiliation in increasingly specific geographic and physical detail.
context.historical: The named cities, Memphis, Zoan, Thebes, Pelusium, On, and others, were major religious, administrative, and military centers throughout Egypt, meaning the judgment described here reaches the entire length of the Nile, not merely a single capital; the second oracle is dated to the eleventh year of Ezekiel's exile, several months after Jerusalem's fall, closely tracking real Babylonian military pressure on Egypt in this period.
context.canonical: Continues the Egypt sequence begun in chapter 29 and stands alongside Isaiah 19 and Jeremiah 46 as part of Scripture's substantial witness to Egypt's judgment, while its 'arm of the LORD' imagery anticipates Isaiah 53's climactic use of the same picture for the suffering servant.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare, in increasingly specific and geographically comprehensive terms, that Egypt's pride and her allies' complacency will be met by a day of the LORD's own reckoning, and to picture Pharaoh's military strength as already broken beyond any human power to restore.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical judgment on Egypt, her allies, and her named cities; it does not prescribe how to relate to modern political alliances. Its abiding principle is that human strength, however impressive, cannot be relied upon or repaired once God has determined to break it, and that no ally can save what God has appointed to fall.

debates.question: Why does the 'Day of the LORD,' a phrase elsewhere strongly tied to Israel's own accountability before God, get applied here to Egypt, a nation outside God's covenant with Israel?

views.label: The Day of the LORD is fundamentally universal in scope, with Israel as one case among many
views.case: Throughout the prophets, the Day of the LORD describes God's decisive intervention wherever pride or injustice has ripened for judgment, and its application to Egypt, Edom, Babylon, and other nations across Scripture shows the phrase was never meant to describe Israel's accountability exclusively, but God's comprehensive lordship over every nation's history.

views.label: The phrase is extended to Egypt specifically to show that Israel's own Day of the LORD experience becomes the pattern applied to all nations
views.case: Because Israel had already experienced a 'day of the LORD' in Jerusalem's fall, chapters 1 to 24, using the same weighty phrase for Egypt deliberately connects Egypt's judgment to Israel's own, showing that the same standard of accountability that fell on God's covenant people now falls, without exception, on a nation entirely outside that covenant.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that no nation, covenant or otherwise, stands outside the reach of God's day of reckoning, and that consistent, universal justice is exactly what this repeated phrase, applied here to Egypt, is meant to communicate.

study.originalLanguages: This chapter calls Egypt's judgment the yom YHWH, the Day of the LORD, language elsewhere tied especially to Israel's own accountability but here turned outward, showing every nation stands under it. And Pharaoh's zeroa, his arm, pictured elsewhere across Scripture as military strength, is declared broken beyond any human splint's power to mend, while God Himself strengthens Babylon's arm for His own purposes.

typology.type: Pharaoh's broken arm, beyond human splint or bandage to heal
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the truth that all human strength, 'the arm of flesh,' fails, while only the true arm of the LORD saves, a phrase Isaiah applies directly to the Messiah, Isaiah 53:1, fulfilled in Christ's unbreakable, saving strength.

study.christ: Pharaoh's arm, once Egypt's proud symbol of military might, is broken beyond any human power to heal or splint. Isaiah later asks 'to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed', Isaiah 53:1, a question answered fully in Christ, the true and only arm of salvation, whose strength, unlike every Pharaoh's, cannot be broken and needs no human effort to sustain it.
study.god: God is Lord over the whole sweep of history, personally breaking one nation's power while personally strengthening another's, even a pagan empire like Babylon, entirely for His own sovereign purposes. His justice is neither passive nor abstract; He names cities one by one and declares, in the first person, exactly whose arm He breaks and whose arm He strengthens.

crossReferences.note: A companion oracle against Egypt from roughly the same period, sharing themes of judgment on Egypt's pride and power.
Asks 'to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed,' a question this chapter's broken 'arm of Pharaoh' throws into sharp relief, answered fully in Christ.
A parallel prophecy against Egypt describing similar themes of pride, defeat, and Babylonian conquest.
Uses similarly weighty 'Day of the LORD' and 'day of clouds' language for coming judgment on the nations generally.

study.oneStory: Egypt's proud strength and all her allies fall on the day the LORD Himself declares, her king's arm broken beyond human healing while Babylon's arm is strengthened for God's own purposes. This anticipates the whole biblical truth that every human arm of strength eventually fails, while the true arm of the LORD, revealed fully in Christ, alone accomplishes a salvation that cannot be broken.

quiz.q: Which nations are named as falling alongside Egypt in this chapter's opening oracle?

options.text: Cush, Put, Lud, Libya, and the men of the covenant land.
options.note: Verse 5 names exactly these allied peoples falling with Egypt by the sword.

options.text: Only Israel and Judah.
options.note: Israel and Judah are not the nations named as falling with Egypt here; the allies named are Cush, Put, Lud, and Libya.

options.text: Assyria and Persia alone.
options.note: Neither is named specifically in this chapter's list of Egypt's falling allies.

options.text: Only the Philistines.
options.note: The Philistines are not the allies named in this passage; the text specifies Cush, Put, Lud, and Libya.

quiz.q: What phrase does the LORD use in verse 3 to describe the coming judgment on Egypt and the nations?

options.text: The Day of the LORD, a day of clouds and a time of doom for the nations.
options.note: Verse 3 uses this exact phrase, language elsewhere often tied to Israel's own accountability but here applied to the nations broadly.

options.text: The year of Jubilee.
options.note: Jubilee is a term for release and restoration, the opposite of the judgment language used in this verse.

options.text: The feast of harvest.
options.note: Not the phrase used; verse 3 speaks of judgment and doom, not a harvest festival.

options.text: The season of exile alone, without further explanation.
options.note: The specific, weighty phrase used is 'the Day of the LORD,' not simply 'exile.'

quiz.q: What happens to the idols and images of Memphis, according to verse 13?

options.text: The LORD declares He will destroy them and put an end to them, along with removing any prince from Egypt.
options.note: Verse 13 states this directly regarding Memphis's idols and Egypt's princely rule.

options.text: They are moved safely to Thebes for protection.
options.note: No such relocation is described; the text describes destruction, not preservation elsewhere.

options.text: They are left untouched while only the city walls are destroyed.
options.note: The text specifically targets the idols and images themselves for destruction, not merely the walls.

options.text: They are given to Babylon as tribute rather than destroyed.
options.note: The text describes destruction of the idols, not their transfer as tribute.

quiz.q: What does the image of Pharaoh's broken arm, verses 20 to 22, communicate?

options.text: That Pharaoh's military strength is already broken beyond any human power to heal, and God will break it further.
options.note: Verses 21 to 22 explicitly describe the arm as unhealed and unsplinted, soon to be broken entirely.

options.text: That Pharaoh had suffered a literal accidental injury unrelated to any battle.
options.note: The 'arm' functions as a symbol of military and political strength throughout Scripture, not merely a literal medical case.

options.text: That Pharaoh's strength was actually growing stronger during this period.
options.note: The opposite is described; his strength, pictured as a broken arm, is declared already failing and beyond repair.

options.text: That Egypt's economy alone was affected, with no impact on her military power.
options.note: The 'arm' image specifically points to strength to 'hold the sword,' a clearly military meaning, not merely economic.

quiz.q: Whose arm does the LORD say He will strengthen in place of Pharaoh's?

options.text: The arms of the king of Babylon, placing His own sword in that king's hand.
options.note: Verses 24 to 25 state this explicitly, contrasting Babylon's strengthened arm with Pharaoh's broken one.

options.text: The arms of the Egyptian priesthood.
options.note: Not mentioned in this passage; the strengthened arm belongs to Babylon's king, not Egyptian priests.

options.text: The arms of Israel's own king.
options.note: Israel's king is not named here as the beneficiary; the text specifies Babylon's king receiving strengthened arms.

options.text: No one; both Egypt's and Babylon's strength are said to fail equally.
options.note: The text draws a clear contrast, Babylon's arms strengthened, Pharaoh's broken, not an equal failure of both.

questions: This chapter applies the weighty phrase 'the Day of the LORD' to Egypt, a nation outside God's covenant with Israel. Where do you need to remember that God's standards of justice and accountability apply universally, not only to people who claim to follow Him?

City after city in Egypt is named individually in this judgment; nothing is too specific or too distant for God's attention. Is there an area of your own life you assume is too small or too hidden for God to be concerned with?
Pharaoh's arm is described as already broken, beyond human power to splint or heal. What strength or ability are you leaning on that may already be failing, whether you have noticed it yet or not?

God strengthens Babylon's arm, a pagan king's, for His own purposes, even though Babylon does not know Him. Where have you seen God use unexpected people or circumstances, even ones that seemed opposed to your good, to accomplish something of His purpose in your life?

Isaiah later asks, 'to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed,' a question answered in Christ's unbreakable strength. Where in your life this week do you need to lean on the arm of the LORD rather than your own arm of flesh?

translationNotes.note: The phrase 'men of the covenant land' likely refers to Jewish mercenaries or settlers known to have lived in Egypt during this period, rather than to Israel or Judah as a whole nation.

Zoan, Thebes, Pelusium, On, Pi-beseth, and Tahpanhes are all identifiable major cities across the length of Egypt, from the Nile delta in the north to Upper Egypt in the south, showing the judgment described reaches the entire land, not one region alone.

The image of an unhealed, unsplinted arm assumes the ancient practice of binding a broken limb with a bandage or splint for it to mend properly; the text specifies this normal remedy has not been applied, meaning the injury is left to worsen rather than heal.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Ezekiel 30:1-26. No verified sermon from the trusted cluster directly expounding this specific passage was found this session; sermon status is none.
